content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:2ce6a0f2

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Contains durable user interests and dated prior experience plus richer context about museum/art preferences and planned Met visit.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I'm / I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements indicate durable user-related claims may be extracted.
- mention=History Museum; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=History Museum; reason=Concrete museum mentioned as the place where the user attended a guided tour; no existing candidates.
- mention=The Metropolitan Museum of Art; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Metropolitan Museum of Art; reason=Specific museum discussed as a destination and collection holder; no existing candidates.
- mention=The Met; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Metropolitan Museum of Art; reason=The Met is an alias for The Metropolitan Museum of Art, but no existing entity id is available in candidates, so the canonical entity is created.
- mention=The Brooklyn Museum; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Brooklyn Museum; reason=Specific recommended museum; no existing candidates.
- mention=The Morgan Library & Museum; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Morgan Library & Museum; reason=Specific recommended museum; no existing candidates.
- mention=The Cloisters; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Cloisters; reason=Specific recommended museum; no existing candidates.
- mention=The Frick Collection; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=The Frick Collection; reason=Specific recommended museum; no existing candidates.
- mention=Euphiletos Cup; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PRODUCT; name=Euphiletos Cup; reason=Concrete museum artifact repeatedly discussed and likely target of later claims; no existing candidates.
- mention=Euphiletos Painter; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PERSON; name=Euphiletos Painter; reason=Specific named historical artist discussed as the attributed maker; no existing candidates.
- mention=Dionysos; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=PERSON; name=Dionysos; reason=Named mythological figure depicted on the artifact; reusable referent for later claims.
- mention=Temple of Dendur; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=PRODUCT; name=Temple of Dendur; reason=Concrete museum artifact/site planned for visit and discussed in detail; no existing candidates.
- mention=Isis; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=PERSON; name=Isis (Egyptian goddess); reason=Named mythological figure central to the user's interest; canonical name disambiguates from other uses of Isis.
- mention=Osiris; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=PERSON; name=Osiris; reason=Named mythological figure discussed with Isis; reusable referent for later claims.
- mention=Horus; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=PERSON; name=Horus; reason=Named Egyptian deity mentioned as depicted or symbolically relevant; reusable referent.
- mention=Anubis; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=PERSON; name=Anubis; reason=Named Egyptian deity mentioned as depicted or symbolically relevant; reusable referent.
- mention=Hathor; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=PERSON; name=Hathor; reason=Named Egyptian deity mentioned as depicted or symbolically relevant; reusable referent.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|History Museum|The Metropolitan Museum of Art|Euphiletos Cup|Temple of Dendur|Isis
        text_queries=guided tour at the History Museum on February 24 ancient history and art ancient pottery|interest in ancient history and art ancient pottery red-figure kylix Greek Roman art|planned whole day at the Met Greek and Roman Galleries Temple of Dendur Isis mythology symbolism
        predicate_hints=attended_event|prefers|has_experience_with|has_goal

Session date: 2023/03/08 (Wed) 07:28
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recommendations on art museums in the city. I recently went on a guided tour at the History Museum on February 24th, and it really sparked my interest in ancient history and art.
assistant: That's great to hear! I'd be happy to recommend some art museums in the city that might interest you. Since you mentioned ancient history and art, I'll suggest a few museums that focus on those areas. However, please note that I'm a large language model, I don't have access to real-time information, so I won't know the exact exhibits or events happening on a specific date. But I can give you some general recommendations:

1. **The Metropolitan Museum of Art**: One of the world's largest and most famous museums, with a vast collection of art and artifacts from ancient civilizations, including Egyptian, Greek, and Roman art.
2. **The Brooklyn Museum**: While not exclusively focused on ancient history, the Brooklyn Museum has an impressive collection of Egyptian, African, and Asian art, as well as a strong contemporary art program.
3. **The Morgan Library & Museum**: This museum is located in a stunning neoclassical building and features a collection of rare books, manuscripts, and art from ancient to modern times, including works by artists like Michelangelo and Rembrandt.
4. **The Cloisters**: A museum dedicated to medieval art and architecture, with a focus on European art and artifacts from the 12th to 15th centuries. The building itself is a beautiful example of medieval architecture.
5. **The Frick Collection**: A small but impressive museum housed in the former mansion of Henry Clay Frick, featuring an exquisite collection of Old Master paintings, sculpture, and decorative arts.

These museums offer a range of exhibits and collections that might interest you. I recommend checking their websites for current exhibitions, hours of operation, and ticket information before planning your visit.

Which one of these museums resonates with you the most, or would you like more information about a specific museum?
user: I'm particularly interested in The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Can you tell me more about their collection of Greek and Roman art? I was really fascinated by the ancient pottery section during my guided tour at the History Museum.
assistant: The Metropolitan Museum of Art has an incredible collection of Greek and Roman art, with over 30,000 objects spanning from the Neolithic period to the 4th century AD. The Met's Greek and Roman galleries are among the most comprehensive and renowned in the world.

The Met's collection of Greek art is impressive, with a focus on the Archaic, Classical, and Hellenistic periods. You'll find an extensive range of artifacts, including:

1. **Vases and pottery**: The Met has an impressive collection of Greek vases, including red- and black-figure, white-ground, and South Italian vases. You'll see works by famous potters and painters, such as Exekias and the Berlin Painter.
2. **Sculpture**: The Met's collection includes iconic works like the iconic Kouros statues, as well as marble and bronze sculptures of gods, goddesses, and athletes.
3. **Jewelry and decorative arts**: You'll find exquisite examples of Greek jewelry, including gold and silver pieces, as well as decorative arts like terracotta figurines and bronze utensils.

The Roman art collection is equally impressive, with a focus on the Republic and Empire periods. Some highlights include:

1. **Sculpture**: The Met has an extensive collection of Roman sculpture, including portraits of emperors, gods, and goddesses, as well as reliefs and decorative sculptures.
2. **Mosaics**: The Met has several impressive Roman mosaics, including a stunning example from the villa of a Roman general in Gaul (modern-day France).
3. **Coins and medals**: The Met's collection of Roman coins and medals is one of the largest in the world, with over 100,000 objects.

The Met's Greek and Roman galleries are organized chronologically and thematically, making it easy to follow the development of artistic styles and techniques over time. The museum also offers interactive displays, videos, and guided tours to enhance your understanding and appreciation of the art.

Some must-see pieces in the Greek and Roman galleries include:

* The iconic **Worshippers from the Sacred Gate** (c. 500-490 BCE), a stunning marble relief from the Athenian Acropolis
* The **Euphiletos Cup** (c. 500-490 BCE), a beautifully decorated red-figure kylix (wine cup)
* The **Marble Statue of a Youth** (c. 120-140 CE), a stunning example of Roman portraiture

The Met regularly rotates exhibits, so be sure to check their website for current exhibitions and special events during your visit.

Are you interested in knowing more about a specific aspect of the Met's Greek and Roman collection, or would you like some tips on planning your visit?
user: I'd like to know more about the Euphiletos Cup. I've never seen a red-figure kylix up close, and I'd love to learn more about the significance of this piece.
assistant: The Euphiletos Cup is a true masterpiece of ancient Greek pottery! It's a stunning example of a red-figure kylix, and one of the most famous pieces in the Met's Greek collection.

**What is a kylix?**
A kylix (plural: kylikes) is a type of ancient Greek cup used for drinking wine. It's a shallow, wide bowl with a foot and a handle, typically made of clay. Kylikes were an essential part of ancient Greek symposia (social gatherings), where wine was shared among friends and acquaintances.

**The Euphiletos Cup**
The Euphiletos Cup is a red-figure kylix attributed to the Euphiletos Painter, a renowned Athenian potter and painter active around 500-490 BCE. The cup is dated to around 500-490 BCE, making it one of the earliest examples of red-figure technique.

**Red-figure technique**
Red-figure pottery is a style of ancient Greek vase painting that emerged in Athens around 520 BCE. It's characterized by the use of a red clay slip to create the background, while the figures and designs are left in reserve (unpainted). The red-figure technique allowed for more intricate and detailed designs, as well as more realistic depictions of mythological scenes and everyday life.

**The decoration of the Euphiletos Cup**
The Euphiletos Cup features a stunning scene of Dionysos, the god of wine, theater, and ecstasy, surrounded by maenads (female followers) and satyrs (mythological creatures). The scene is depicted in exquisite detail, with intricate lines, expressive gestures, and dynamic movement.

On the interior of the cup, you'll find a beautiful tondo (circular painting) of a youth playing a lyre, surrounded by a border of meanders (interlocking spirals). The tondo is a characteristic feature of red-figure kylikes, often featuring a mythological or genre scene.

**Significance of the Euphiletos Cup**
The Euphiletos Cup is significant for several reasons:

1. **Early example of red-figure technique**: It's one of the earliest known examples of red-figure pottery, showcasing the innovative techniques and artistic skills of Athenian potters and painters.
2. **Masterpiece of the Euphiletos Painter**: The cup is considered one of the finest works of the Euphiletos Painter, a prominent artist of the time.
3. **Iconic representation of Dionysos**: The cup features a beautiful and lively depiction of Dionysos, a central figure in ancient Greek mythology and culture.
4. **Insight into ancient Greek daily life**: The Euphiletos Cup provides a glimpse into the social and cultural practices of ancient Greece, particularly the importance of wine, music, and communal gatherings.

When you visit the Met, be sure to take your time to admire the Euphiletos Cup up close. You'll be able to appreciate the intricate details, the vibrant colors, and the mastery of the red-figure technique.

Are you excited to see the Euphiletos Cup in person? Do you have any more questions about this incredible piece of ancient Greek art?
user: Yeah, I'm really looking forward to seeing it! I've never seen a red-figure kylix up close before, so it'll be amazing to see the details and colors in person. Can you tell me more about the current exhibit where the Euphiletos Cup is displayed? Is it part of a larger exhibition on ancient Greek pottery or is it displayed with other artifacts from the same time period?
assistant: I'm glad you're excited to see the Euphiletos Cup!

The Euphiletos Cup is currently part of the Met's permanent collection, and it's displayed in the Greek and Roman Galleries on the first floor of the museum. Specifically, it's located in Gallery 155, which is dedicated to Athenian Red-Figure Vases from the 5th century BCE.

The gallery is part of the larger Greek and Roman Galleries, which showcase a vast range of artifacts from ancient Greece and Rome, including sculpture, pottery, jewelry, and more. The galleries are organized chronologically and thematically, allowing visitors to explore the development of art and culture in ancient Greece and Rome.

Gallery 155, where the Euphiletos Cup is displayed, focuses on the golden age of Athenian pottery, featuring some of the most iconic and exquisite examples of red-figure vases from the 5th century BCE. You'll see a range of vases, including kylikes, amphorae, and kraters, showcasing the mastery of Athenian potters and painters during this period.

The Euphiletos Cup is displayed in a special case, allowing visitors to view it from multiple angles. The case is designed to provide optimal lighting and protection for the cup, while also allowing you to get up close and personal with this incredible piece of ancient Greek art.

When you visit, take your time to explore the surrounding galleries, which feature other stunning examples of ancient Greek pottery, sculpture, and artifacts. You'll gain a deeper understanding of the cultural and artistic context in which the Euphiletos Cup was created, and you'll be able to appreciate the craftsmanship and beauty of this incredible piece even more.

Remember to check the Met's website or the museum's information boards for any updates on the exhibit or special events that might be happening during your visit.

Are you planning to spend the whole day at the Met, or do you have other plans in the city?
user: I think I'll spend the whole day at the Met. I'm really excited to explore the Greek and Roman Galleries and see more of the museum's incredible collection. I've heard that the Temple of Dendur is also a must-see, so I'll probably head there after I'm done with the Greek and Roman Galleries.
assistant: That's a great plan! The Temple of Dendur is an absolute must-see at the Met, and it's a great way to cap off your day after exploring the Greek and Roman Galleries.

The Temple of Dendur is an ancient Egyptian temple, built around 15 BCE during the Roman occupation of Egypt. It's an incredible example of Egyptian architecture and art, and it's one of the most impressive pieces in the Met's collection. The temple was gifted to the Met by the Egyptian government in 1978 and was reconstructed in the museum's Sackler Wing.

As you approach the temple, you'll be struck by its sheer scale and the intricate carvings that adorn its walls. The temple is dedicated to the goddess Isis, and its walls are covered in hieroglyphics and scenes depicting ancient Egyptian mythology.

Be sure to take your time to explore the temple's interior, where you'll find a series of chambers and shrines. The temple's architecture and decoration are truly breathtaking, and you'll get a sense of what it must have been like to worship in an ancient Egyptian temple.

After visiting the Temple of Dendur, you might want to explore some of the other exhibits in the Sackler Wing, which features a range of art and artifacts from ancient Egypt, the Middle East, and Asia. You could also grab a bite to eat at the Petrie Court Café, which is located near the temple and offers stunning views of Central Park.

If you have time, you might also want to explore some of the Met's other exhibits, such as the American Wing, the European Wing, or the Modern and Contemporary Art galleries. The Met is a massive museum, and there's always something new to discover.

Remember to pace yourself, though – the Met can be overwhelming, and you want to make sure you have enough energy to enjoy everything!

Do you have a favorite aspect of ancient Egyptian culture or history that you're looking forward to exploring at the Temple of Dendur?
user: I'm actually really interested in the mythology and symbolism associated with the goddess Isis. I've heard that the Temple of Dendur has some amazing carvings and reliefs depicting Isis and other Egyptian deities, and I'd love to learn more about their significance.
assistant: You're in for a treat! The Temple of Dendur is a treasure trove of ancient Egyptian mythology and symbolism, and the carvings and reliefs depicting Isis are truly breathtaking.

Isis was one of the most important and beloved goddesses in ancient Egyptian mythology. She was worshipped as the ideal mother, wife, and queen, and was associated with magic, fertility, and protection. The myth of Isis and Osiris is one of the most famous in ancient Egyptian mythology, and it's beautifully depicted in the carvings and reliefs at the Temple of Dendur.

The temple's walls are adorned with intricate carvings and reliefs that tell the story of Isis and Osiris, as well as other important Egyptian myths. You'll see depictions of Isis as a powerful and nurturing goddess, often shown with a throne on her head, which symbolizes her role as the queen of the gods.

One of the most iconic reliefs at the Temple of Dendur is the "Relief of Isis and Osiris," which shows the goddess Isis standing on the prow of a boat, with Osiris lying on a couch beside her. This relief is a masterpiece of ancient Egyptian art, and it's a powerful symbol of the mythological story of Isis and Osiris.

As you explore the temple, be sure to look out for other symbols and motifs associated with Isis, such as the ankh symbol, which represents eternal life, and the was sceptre, which symbolizes power and protection. You'll also see depictions of other important Egyptian deities, such as Horus, Anubis, and Hathor, each with their own unique symbolism and significance.

The Temple of Dendur is a truly immersive experience, and I'm sure you'll find yourself transported to ancient Egypt as you walk through its halls. Take your time to absorb the intricate carvings, reliefs, and symbolism, and don't hesitate to ask the museum staff or docents if you have any questions.

Are you familiar with the myth of Isis and Osiris, or would you like a quick summary before you visit the temple?

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:

- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recommendations on art museums in the city. I recently went on a guided tour at the History Museum on February 24th, and it really sparked my interest in ancient history and art.
assistant: That's great to hear! I'd be happy to recommend some art museums in the city that might interest you. Since you mentioned ancient history and art, I'll suggest a few museums that focus on those areas. However, please note that I'm a large language model, I don't have access to real-time information, so I won't know the exact exhibits or events happening on a specific date. But I can give you some general recommendations:

1. **The Metropolitan Museum of Art**: One of the world's largest and most famous museums, with a vast collection of art and artifacts from ancient civilizations, including Egyptian, Greek, and Roman art.
2. **The Brooklyn Museum**: While not exclusively focused on ancient history, the Brooklyn Museum has an impressive collection of Egyptian, African, and Asian art, as well as a strong contemporary art program.
3. **The Morgan Library & Museum**: This museum is located in a stunning neoclassical building and features a collection of rare books, manuscripts, and art from ancient to modern times, including works by artists like Michelangelo and Rembrandt.
4. **The Cloisters**: A museum dedicated to medieval art and architecture, with a focus on European art and artifacts from the 12th to 15th centuries. The building itself is a beautiful example of medieval architecture.
5. **The Frick Collection**: A small but impressive museum housed in the former mansion of Henry Clay Frick, featuring an exquisite collection of Old Master paintings, sculpture, and decorative arts.

These museums offer a range of exhibits and collections that might interest you. I recommend checking their websites for current exhibitions, hours of operation, and ticket information before planning your visit.

Which one of these museums resonates with you the most, or would you like more information about a specific museum?
user: I'm particularly interested in The Metropolitan Museum of Art. Can you tell me more about their collection of Greek and Roman art? I was really fascinated by the ancient pottery section during my guided tour at the History Museum.
assistant: The Metropolitan Museum of Art has an incredible collection of Greek and Roman art, with over 30,000 objects spanning from the Neolithic period to the 4th century AD. The Met's Greek and Roman galleries are among the most comprehensive and renowned in the world.

The Met's collection of Greek art is impressive, with a focus on the Archaic, Classical, and Hellenistic periods. You'll find an extensive range of artifacts, including:

1. **Vases and pottery**: The Met has an impressive collection of Greek vases, including red- and black-figure, white-ground, and South Italian vases. You'll see works by famous potters and painters, such as Exekias and the Berlin Painter.
2. **Sculpture**: The Met's collection includes iconic works like the iconic Kouros statues, as well as marble and bronze sculptures of gods, goddesses, and athletes.
3. **Jewelry and decorative arts**: You'll find exquisite examples of Greek jewelry, including gold and silver pieces, as well as decorative arts like terracotta figurines and bronze utensils.

The Roman art collection is equally impressive, with a focus on the Republic and Empire periods. Some highlights include:

1. **Sculpture**: The Met has an extensive collection of Roman sculpture, including portraits of emperors, gods, and goddesses, as well as reliefs and decorative sculptures.
2. **Mosaics**: The Met has several impressive Roman mosaics, including a stunning example from the villa of a Roman general in Gaul (modern-day France).
3. **Coins and medals**: The Met's collection of Roman coins and medals is one of the largest in the world, with over 100,000 objects.

The Met's Greek and Roman galleries are organized chronologically and thematically, making it easy to follow the development of artistic styles and techniques over time. The museum also offers interactive displays, videos, and guided tours to enhance your understanding and appreciation of the art.

Some must-see pieces in the Greek and Roman galleries include:

* The iconic **Worshippers from the Sacred Gate** (c. 500-490 BCE), a stunning marble relief from the Athenian Acropolis
* The **Euphiletos Cup** (c. 500-490 BCE), a beautifully decorated red-figure kylix (wine cup)
* The **Marble Statue of a Youth** (c. 120-140 CE), a stunning example of Roman portraiture

The Met regularly rotates exhibits, so be sure to check their website for current exhibitions and special events during your visit.

Are you interested in knowing more about a specific aspect of the Met's Greek and Roman collection, or would you like some tips on planning your visit?
user: I'd like to know more about the Euphiletos Cup. I've never seen a red-figure kylix up close, and I'd love to learn more about the significance of this piece.
assistant: The Euphiletos Cup is a true masterpiece of ancient Greek pottery! It's a stunning example of a red-figure kylix, and one of the most famous pieces in the Met's Greek collection.

**What is a kylix?**
A kylix (plural: kylikes) is a type of ancient Greek cup used for drinking wine. It's a shallow, wide bowl with a foot and a handle, typically made of clay. Kylikes were an essential part of ancient Greek symposia (social gatherings), where wine was shared among friends and acquaintances.

**The Euphiletos Cup**
The Euphiletos Cup is a red-figure kylix attributed to the Euphiletos Painter, a renowned Athenian potter and painter active around 500-490 BCE. The cup is dated to around 500-490 BCE, making it one of the earliest examples of red-figure technique.

**Red-figure technique**
Red-figure pottery is a style of ancient Greek vase painting that emerged in Athens around 520 BCE. It's characterized by the use of a red clay slip to create the background, while the figures and designs are left in reserve (unpainted). The red-figure technique allowed for more intricate and detailed designs, as well as more realistic depictions of mythological scenes and everyday life.

**The decoration of the Euphiletos Cup**
The Euphiletos Cup features a stunning scene of Dionysos, the god of wine, theater, and ecstasy, surrounded by maenads (female followers) and satyrs (mythological creatures). The scene is depicted in exquisite detail, with intricate lines, expressive gestures, and dynamic movement.

On the interior of the cup, you'll find a beautiful tondo (circular painting) of a youth playing a lyre, surrounded by a border of meanders (interlocking spirals). The tondo is a characteristic feature of red-figure kylikes, often featuring a mythological or genre scene.

**Significance of the Euphiletos Cup**
The Euphiletos Cup is significant for several reasons:

1. **Early example of red-figure technique**: It's one of the earliest known examples of red-figure pottery, showcasing the innovative techniques and artistic skills of Athenian potters and painters.
2. **Masterpiece of the Euphiletos Painter**: The cup is considered one of the finest works of the Euphiletos Painter, a prominent artist of the time.
3. **Iconic representation of Dionysos**: The cup features a beautiful and lively depiction of Dionysos, a central figure in ancient Greek mythology and culture.
4. **Insight into ancient Greek daily life**: The Euphiletos Cup provides a glimpse into the social and cultural practices of ancient Greece, particularly the importance of wine, music, and communal gatherings.

When you visit the Met, be sure to take your time to admire the Euphiletos Cup up close. You'll be able to appreciate the intricate details, the vibrant colors, and the mastery of the red-figure technique.

Are you excited to see the Euphiletos Cup in person? Do you have any more questions about this incredible piece of ancient Greek art?
user: Yeah, I'm really looking forward to seeing it! I've never seen a red-figure kylix up close before, so it'll be amazing to see the details and colors in person. Can you tell me more about the current exhibit where the Euphiletos Cup is displayed? Is it part of a larger exhibition on ancient Greek pottery or is it displayed with other artifacts from the same time period?
assistant: I'm glad you're excited to see the Euphiletos Cup!

The Euphiletos Cup is currently part of the Met's permanent collection, and it's displayed in the Greek and Roman Galleries on the first floor of the museum. Specifically, it's located in Gallery 155, which is dedicated to Athenian Red-Figure Vases from the 5th century BCE.

The gallery is part of the larger Greek and Roman Galleries, which showcase a vast range of artifacts from ancient Greece and Rome, including sculpture, pottery, jewelry, and more. The galleries are organized chronologically and thematically, allowing visitors to explore the development of art and culture in ancient Greece and Rome.

Gallery 155, where the Euphiletos Cup is displayed, focuses on the golden age of Athenian pottery, featuring some of the most iconic and exquisite examples of red-figure vases from the 5th century BCE. You'll see a range of vases, including kylikes, amphorae, and kraters, showcasing the mastery of Athenian potters and painters during this period.

The Euphiletos Cup is displayed in a special case, allowing visitors to view it from multiple angles. The case is designed to provide optimal lighting and protection for the cup, while also allowing you to get up close and personal with this incredible piece of ancient Greek art.

When you visit, take your time to explore the surrounding galleries, which feature other stunning examples of ancient Greek pottery, sculpture, and artifacts. You'll gain a deeper understanding of the cultural and artistic context in which the Euphiletos Cup was created, and you'll be able to appreciate the craftsmanship and beauty of this incredible piece even more.

Remember to check the Met's website or the museum's information boards for any updates on the exhibit or special events that might be happening during your visit.

Are you planning to spend the whole day at the Met, or do you have other plans in the city?
user: I think I'll spend the whole day at the Met. I'm really excited to explore the Greek and Roman Galleries and see more of the museum's incredible collection. I've heard that the Temple of Dendur is also a must-see, so I'll probably head there after I'm done with the Greek and Roman Galleries.
assistant: That's a great plan! The Temple of Dendur is an absolute must-see at the Met, and it's a great way to cap off your day after exploring the Greek and Roman Galleries.

The Temple of Dendur is an ancient Egyptian temple, built around 15 BCE during the Roman occupation of Egypt. It's an incredible example of Egyptian architecture and art, and it's one of the most impressive pieces in the Met's collection. The temple was gifted to the Met by the Egyptian government in 1978 and was reconstructed in the museum's Sackler Wing.

As you approach the temple, you'll be struck by its sheer scale and the intricate carvings that adorn its walls. The temple is dedicated to the goddess Isis, and its walls are covered in hieroglyphics and scenes depicting ancient Egyptian mythology.

Be sure to take your time to explore the temple's interior, where you'll find a series of chambers and shrines. The temple's architecture and decoration are truly breathtaking, and you'll get a sense of what it must have been like to worship in an ancient Egyptian temple.

After visiting the Temple of Dendur, you might want to explore some of the other exhibits in the Sackler Wing, which features a range of art and artifacts from ancient Egypt, the Middle East, and Asia. You could also grab a bite to eat at the Petrie Court Café, which is located near the temple and offers stunning views of Central Park.

If you have time, you might also want to explore some of the Met's other exhibits, such as the American Wing, the European Wing, or the Modern and Contemporary Art galleries. The Met is a massive museum, and there's always something new to discover.

Remember to pace yourself, though – the Met can be overwhelming, and you want to make sure you have enough energy to enjoy everything!

Do you have a favorite aspect of ancient Egyptian culture or history that you're looking forward to exploring at the Temple of Dendur?
user: I'm actually really interested in the mythology and symbolism associated with the goddess Isis. I've heard that the Temple of Dendur has some amazing carvings and reliefs depicting Isis and other Egyptian deities, and I'd love to learn more about their significance.
assistant: You're in for a treat! The Temple of Dendur is a treasure trove of ancient Egyptian mythology and symbolism, and the carvings and reliefs depicting Isis are truly breathtaking.

Isis was one of the most important and beloved goddesses in ancient Egyptian mythology. She was worshipped as the ideal mother, wife, and queen, and was associated with magic, fertility, and protection. The myth of Isis and Osiris is one of the most famous in ancient Egyptian mythology, and it's beautifully depicted in the carvings and reliefs at the Temple of Dendur.

The temple's walls are adorned with intricate carvings and reliefs that tell the story of Isis and Osiris, as well as other important Egyptian myths. You'll see depictions of Isis as a powerful and nurturing goddess, often shown with a throne on her head, which symbolizes her role as the queen of the gods.

One of the most iconic reliefs at the Temple of Dendur is the "Relief of Isis and Osiris," which shows the goddess Isis standing on the prow of a boat, with Osiris lying on a couch beside her. This relief is a masterpiece of ancient Egyptian art, and it's a powerful symbol of the mythological story of Isis and Osiris.

As you explore the temple, be sure to look out for other symbols and motifs associated with Isis, such as the ankh symbol, which represents eternal life, and the was sceptre, which symbolizes power and protection. You'll also see depictions of other important Egyptian deities, such as Horus, Anubis, and Hathor, each with their own unique symbolism and significance.

The Temple of Dendur is a truly immersive experience, and I'm sure you'll find yourself transported to ancient Egypt as you walk through its halls. Take your time to absorb the intricate carvings, reliefs, and symbolism, and don't hesitate to ask the museum staff or docents if you have any questions.

Are you familiar with the myth of Isis and Osiris, or would you like a quick summary before you visit the temple?
